lesson.name: Analysis Applications - Lesson 5: Mathematical Analysis and Applications

tasks.chapterTitle: Periodically Forced Nonlinear Oscillatory Acoustic Vacuum
Solutions to Abel’s Integral Equations in Distributions
Interior Regularity Estimates for a Degenerate Elliptic Equation
Sub-Optimal Control in the Zika Virus Epidemic Model
Some Identities for Euler and Bernoulli Polynomials and Their Zeros
Asymptotically J-Statistical Equivalence and Pre-Metric Spaces
Yukawa Potential, Panharmonic Measure and Brownian Motion to Summation Theorems

messages.content:
This document is a collection of peer-reviewed research articles published in a Special Issue of the journal *Axioms*. The issue focuses on recent advances in mathematical analysis and their multidisciplinary applications.

### **Key Highlights of the Special Issue**
*   **Editor:** Prof. Hari Mohan Srivastava (University of Victoria, Canada).
*   **Focus:** Theoretical developments in mathematical analysis combined with practical applications in physical, chemical, engineering, and statistical sciences.
*   **Scope:** The collection covers a wide range of topics, including:
    *   **Special Functions:** Examination of Bell–Sheffer polynomial sets, hypergeometric functions, and Euler/Bernoulli polynomials.
    *   **Fractional Calculus:** Studies on integral equations and generalized fractional differintegral operators.
    *   **Applied Analysis:** Non-linear oscillatory acoustic vacuum, non-Fourier heat equations, and Zika virus epidemic modeling using differential evolution.
    *   **Mathematical Theory:** Umbral methods, pre-metric spaces, subordination properties, and statistical convergence.

### **Featured Research (Sample of 7 Selected Works)**
1.  **New Bell–Sheffer Polynomial Sets** (Natalini & Ricci): Explores iterated exponential/logarithmic functions and their connection to combinatorial analysis [S.12].
2.  **Periodically Forced Nonlinear Oscillatory Acoustic Vacuum** (Agaoglou et al.): Investigates non-linear dynamics in acoustic systems [S.6].
3.  **Solutions to Abel’s Integral Equations in Distributions** (Li et al.): Provides distribution-based solutions to integral problems [S.6].
4.  **Interior Regularity Estimates for a Degenerate Elliptic Equation** (Djida & Fernandez): Focuses on boundary conditions in elliptic equations [S.6].
5.  **Umbral Methods and Harmonic Numbers** (Dattoli et al.): Discusses the application of umbral calculus to combinatorial sequences [S.6].
6.  **Sub-Optimal Control in the Zika Virus Epidemic Model** (Chaikham & Sawangtong): Applies evolutionary algorithms to epidemiological modeling [S.6].
7.  **Some Identities for Euler and Bernoulli Polynomials** (Kim & Ryoo): Analyzes the properties and zeros of these classic polynomial families [S.6].

### **Publication Context**
*   **Format:** The volume serves as a high-impact, open-access (CC BY) resource designed to disseminate advanced mathematical research globally [S.5, S.10].
*   **Editorial Standards:** The issue maintained a rigorous process, resulting in the publication of 15 original research articles from an international pool of authors [S.10].

Lesson 5: Mathematical Analysis and Applications - Presentation10 slides
Mathematical Analysis and Applications
  • Overview of diverse research in mathematical analysis.
  • Special issue published in Axioms (2018).
  • Features applications from oscillation theory to fractional calculus.

This document summarizes the Special Issue of the journal *Axioms* titled "Mathematical Analysis and Applications," edited by Hari Mohan Srivastava [S.1, S.4, S.10].

**Overview of the Special Issue**
The volume compiles peer-reviewed research articles that explore the theory and multidisciplinary applications of mathematical analysis. The call for papers focused on several key areas, including:
* Mathematical and higher transcendental functions [S.10].
* Fractional calculus and $q$-series or $q$-polynomials [S.10].
* Analytic number theory [S.10].
* Geometric function theory of complex analysis [S.10].

**Key Statistics and Editorial Details**
* Publications: The issue successfully published 15 research articles [S.10].
* Manuscript Processing: 24 submissions were rejected, with an average processing time of 52 days [S.10].
* Global Reach: International contributions were received from authors across 15 countries, with the highest concentration of authors based in Italy and Turkey [S.10, S.11].
* Licensing: The content is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license to ensure broad accessibility [S.5].

**Editor Profile**
The Special Issue editor, Hari Mohan Srivastava, is a Professor Emeritus at the University of Victoria, Canada. His extensive scholarly career includes over 1100 scientific research articles, 27 books and monographs, and recognition as a Highly Cited Researcher by Clarivate Analytics [S.8].

**Contents and Scope**
The articles cover a broad spectrum of topics, ranging from purely theoretical advancements—such as new Bell–Sheffer polynomial sets [S.6] and summation theorems for generalized hypergeometric functions [S.6]—to applied research, including sub-optimal control in Zika virus epidemic modeling [S.6] and solutions to non-Fourier heat equations [S.6].

The provided document outlines the construction and properties of new "Bell–Sheffer" polynomial sets, which are derived using iterated exponential and logarithmic functions. The work is grounded in the monomiality principle, allowing for an explicit derivation of main polynomial properties.

### Key Conceptual Contributions

**Iterated Functions**
The authors define a sequence of iterated exponential ($E_r(t)$) and logarithmic ($\Lambda_r(t)$) functions. These functions satisfy reciprocal and operational identities such as $E_r(\Lambda_r(t)) = t$. The Taylor coefficients of these functions are identified as higher-order Bell numbers and higher-order logarithmic numbers (p. [S.14]).

**Polynomial Construction**
New Bell–Sheffer polynomial sets $\{E_k^{(r)}(x)\}$ are constructed using the generating function:
$$G(t, x) = \exp[(1+x)E_{r-1}(t)] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} E_k^{(r)}(x) \frac{t^k}{k!}$$
These polynomials generalize standard Sheffer sequences by incorporating the hierarchy of Bell-type functions (p. [S.14], [S.19]).

### Operational Properties

**Quasi-Monomiality**
The sets are classified as quasi-monomial, meaning they are characterized by derivative ($\hat{P}$) and multiplication ($\hat{M}$) operators such that $\hat{P}(p_n(x)) = n p_{n-1}(x)$ and $\hat{M}(p_n(x)) = p_{n+1}(x)$. For the general case of order $r$, the operators are defined as:
$$\hat{P} = \Lambda_{r-1}(D_x)$$
$$\hat{M} = (1+x)(D_x+1) \prod_{k=0}^{r-2} [\Lambda_k(D_x) + 1]$$
These operators allow for the derivation of specific differential equations for each polynomial set (p. [S.17], [S.20]).

**Differential Equations**
By applying the monomiality principle through the condition $\hat{M}\hat{P}p_n(x) = np_n(x)$, the authors derive specific, higher-order homogeneous linear differential equations satisfied by the Bell–Sheffer polynomials (p. [S.17], [S.20]).

**Combinatorial Connections**
Specific cases of these polynomials yield numerical sequences found in the Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences. For instance, the sequence $E_k^{(1)}(0)$ corresponds to the standard Bell numbers ($1, 1, 2, 5, 15, 52, \dots$), while $E_k^{(2)}(0)$ relates to the McLaurin coefficients of the function $E_2(x)$ (p. [S.18], [S.19]).

This article investigates the persistence of periodic oscillations in a finite lattice of particles that act as a low-energy nonlinear acoustic vacuum. The system consists of particles coupled by linear springs, subject to harmonic excitation, damping, and transverse forces [S.22].

### Key Theoretical Findings
The study focuses on a normalized model where transverse oscillations can be decoupled from axial ones in the low-energy limit. These transverse dynamics are described by:
$$\frac{d^2 w_i}{d\tau^2} + \frac{1}{2(N+1)} \left( \sum_{q=0}^{N} (w_{q+1} - w_q)^2 \right) (2w_i - w_{i+1} - w_{i-1}) = 0$$
This system admits nonlinear normal modes (NNMs) that depend on the responses of all other particles due to the presence of a strongly nonlocal multiplier [S.24].

### Analysis of the Two-Mode System
The authors analyze a simplified system incorporating two modes, $k$ and $p$. By applying coordinate transformations and an averaging approach, they derive a periodically-perturbed system governed by variables $\rho$, $\theta$, and $\Delta$ [S.25, S.26].
The unperturbed system ($\epsilon=0$) is integrable, exhibiting periodic solutions with:
$T = \frac{8k^3P^3\pi}{K\rho^2}$ [S.29].

### Melnikov Analysis
To determine the persistence of these periodic oscillations under perturbation, the authors employ Melnikov-type analysis. By defining a Melnikov function $M(\beta_1, \rho)$, they establish conditions for finding simple roots, which correlate to the existence of periodic solutions for the perturbed system.
The main result, Theorem 1, states that under specific conditions for parameters $\beta_1, \rho, \mu_1,$ and $\mu_2$, there exist periodic solutions for sufficiently small perturbations $\epsilon$ [S.30, S.31].

This paper uses the distributional space $D'(R_+)$ and inverse convolutional operators to study fractional calculus, Abel's integral equations, and fractional differential equations. The key points are summarized below:

**Core Concepts and Definitions**
The study utilizes the Schwartz space $D(R)$ and its dual space of distributions $D'(R)$. A central component is the distribution $\Phi_\lambda = \frac{x_+^{\lambda-1}}{\Gamma(\lambda)}$, which behaves as an entire function of $\lambda$ on the complex plane. Fractional derivatives and integrals are defined via distributional convolution using the kernel $\Phi_{-\lambda}$, which allows for representing solutions to integral equations in terms of series or Mittag-Leffler functions (p. 36-38).

**Theorem and Methodology**
The authors present a generalized version of Abel's integral equation:
$G(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \int_0^x (x-\zeta)^{\alpha-1} y(\zeta) d\zeta$
This equation is solved using the inverse convolution operator, where the solution is given by $y(x) = G(x) * \Phi_{-\alpha}$. The authors provide specific solutions depending on the value of $\alpha$ (e.g., cases where $\alpha$ is a positive integer, zero, or negative) (p. 39).

**Applications and Examples**
The paper demonstrates the efficacy of this approach through several examples:
1. It solves integral equations involving delta functions and singular kernels, such as $\int_0^x y(\tau)(x-\tau)^{n/2-1} d\tau = x_+^s + \delta^{(k)}(x)$, which cannot be addressed by standard Laplace transform methods (p. 39-40).
2. It derives closed-form expressions for equations containing trigonometric functions and Mittag-Leffler kernels by utilizing Taylor expansions of kernels within the distributional framework (p. 40-42).
3. The methodology allows for the extension to more complex kernels involving exponential functions, expressed as convergent series (p. 42-43).

**Significance**
The approach is notable because it provides solutions in the distributional sense that are inaccessible via classical numerical analysis or Laplace transforms. It establishes a robust framework for handling fractional models in viscoelastic systems and other non-local dynamic processes (p. 36).

This paper focuses on proving interior Schauder regularity estimates for a degenerate elliptic equation with mixed Dirichlet–Neumann boundary conditions, which relates to the Marchaud fractional derivative.

### Key Objectives and Motivations
The research expands on the connection between nonlocal operators (such as the Marchaud fractional derivative, $(D_{right})^{\alpha}$) and local degenerate partial differential equations (PDEs) via extension problems. Nonlocal operators are essential for modeling memory effects and long-range interactions, but their nonlocality hinders the application of standard nonlinear PDE techniques. By mapping these operators to an extension problem on the upper half-space $\mathbb{R}^{2}_{+}$, the authors enable the use of classical analysis tools.

### The Extension Problem
The core of the study is a degenerate elliptic equation defined by:
$M_{\alpha}U(t, x) = 0$ for $(t, x) \in \mathbb{R}^{2}_{+}$
$\lim_{t \to 0} N_{\alpha}U(t, x) = f(x)$ for $x \in \Omega$
where $M_{\alpha} := -\partial_{x} + \frac{1-2\alpha}{t}\partial_{t} + \partial_{tt}$ and $N_{\alpha} = -t^{1-2\alpha}\partial_{t}$. This framework allows the evaluation of fractional derivatives as Dirichlet-to-Neumann maps, mirroring the approach for the fractional Laplacian established by Caffarelli and Silvestre.

### Main Regulatory Results (Theorem 1)
The authors establish interior Schauder estimates for weak solutions $U$ of the extension problem:
1. For $f \in L^{p}(B_{1}, w)$ and $\gamma \in (0, \min(1, \alpha))$, the solution $U$ belongs to the H\u00f6lder space $C^{0, \alpha-\gamma-1/p}(B^{+}_{1}, w)$.
2. For $f \in L^{\infty}(B_{1})$ and $\gamma \in (0, \min(1, \alpha))$, the solution $U$ belongs to $C^{\alpha-\gamma}(B^{+}_{1})$.

### Methodology and Theoretical Framework
- Function Spaces: The study utilizes weighted $L^{p}$ spaces, where the Muckenhoupt weight $w = t^{1-2\alpha}$ is central to handling the degenerate nature of the PDE.
- Sobolev Spaces: The paper relies on the equivalence between the fractional Sobolev space $H^{\alpha}(\mathbb{R})$ and the trace of functions in $\dot{H}^{1}(t^{\mu}; B)$ on the boundary $t=0$.
- Comparison: The work distinguishes its approach from time-dependent heat conduction problems (e.g., Bucur and Ferrari) by strictly utilizing the Bernardis–Reyes–Stinga–Torrea extension, which maintains the stationary nature of the operator.

(pp. 52-58)

This document details the application of umbral operational methods to harmonic-based functions. By treating harmonic numbers as operators, the authors simplify complex algebraic calculations into elementary manipulations. 

Key points:

Harmonic Numbers and Umbral Framework
The harmonic number $h_n$ is defined by the integral representation $h_n = \int_0^1 \frac{1-x^n}{1-x} dx$ (p. 69). The authors introduce the harmonic number umbral vacuum $\phi_h(z)$ and a shift operator $\hat{h} = e^{\partial_z}$ (p. 69). Applying this operator to the vacuum yields the harmonic numbers, satisfying $\hat{h}^n = h_n$ (p. 69).

Harmonic-Based Exponential Function (HBEF)
The HBEF is defined as $h_e(x) = e^{\hat{h}x} \phi_h(0) = 1 + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{h_n}{n!} x^n$ (p. 70). This function serves as a generating function and obeys specific differential structures (p. 70). Notably, $h_e(x)$ satisfies the non-homogeneous differential equation $h_e'(x) = h_e(x) + \frac{e^x - x - 1}{x}$ (p. 72).

Harmonic Polynomials and Hermite Extensions
Harmonic polynomials are defined by $h_n(x) = (x + \hat{h})^n \phi_h(0)$ (p. 72). These are categorized as Appél polynomials (p. 73). Furthermore, the authors define harmonic Hermite polynomials via the relationship $\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{t^n}{n!} hH_n(x) = e^{xt} h_e(t^2)$ (p. 74).

Extensions
The formalism is extended to truncated exponential numbers $e_n = \sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{r!}$ (p. 74). The authors conclude that these umbral techniques provide a powerful and flexible toolkit for handling series and integrals involving harmonic, generalized, and truncated numbers (p. 71, 74).

This research aims to manage the Zika virus (ZIKV) epidemic using a mathematical dynamical model and a sub-optimal control strategy. The study focuses on three transmission pathways: vector-borne, sexual, and vertical.

Key points from the provided text are as follows:

**Dynamical Model**
The researchers developed a system of differential equations involving three populations: newborn babies, adults, and mosquitoes. The model incorporates specific transmission dynamics:
* **Human-to-Human:** Sexual transmission between adults.
* **Vector-to-Human:** Mosquito bites affecting both adults and babies.
* **Vertical Transmission:** Infection passed from pregnant mothers to babies, including the potential for microcephaly.

**Control Strategy**
To mitigate the epidemic, three control variables are implemented:
* $u_1(t)$: Reducing human-mosquito contact (e.g., insect repellents).
* $u_2(t)$: Reducing sexual transmission (e.g., protection).
* $u_3(t)$: Eliminating vector populations (e.g., insecticides).

The goal is to minimize the objective function $J$, which accounts for the infected populations and the financial cost of implementing these controls (p. 80).

**Computational Method**
Rather than using continuous-time adjustments, which are often impractical for policy implementation, the authors utilize a sub-optimal control approach. The time horizon is discretized into fixed intervals where control levels remain constant. This problem is solved using the Differential Evolution (DE) algorithm, an evolutionary strategy known for its speed, simplicity, and ability to handle non-linear, multi-dimensional optimization problems (p. 81, 82).

**Conclusions**
The simulation results indicate that applying these controls effectively reduces both the number of infected patients and the total duration of the epidemic. Among the strategies tested, vector elimination is identified as the highest priority for epidemic control (p. 77).

This paper explores the properties of cosine-Bernoulli, sine-Bernoulli, cosine-Euler, and sine-Euler polynomials, extending the classical definitions of Bernoulli and Euler polynomials.

**Definitions and Generating Functions**
The authors define these new polynomial sequences based on the Euler and Bernoulli generating functions expanded by trigonometric components. Specifically:
- Cosine-Euler and sine-Euler polynomials are derived from the generating functions involving $\frac{2}{e^t+1}e^{xt}\cos(yt)$ and $\frac{2}{e^t+1}e^{xt}\sin(yt)$ (p. 93).
- Cosine-Bernoulli and sine-Bernoulli polynomials are similarly defined using the generating function $\frac{t}{e^t-1}e^{xt}$ (p. 98).

**Key Identities and Properties**
Several fundamental identities are established for these polynomials:
- Symmetry and Reflection: The paper provides reflection formulas, such as $E^{(C)}_n(1-x, y) = (-1)^n E^{(C)}_n(x, y)$ (p. 95) and $B^{(C)}_n(1-x, y) = (-1)^n B^{(C)}_n(x, y)$ (p. 99).
- Differential Relations: Derivatives with respect to $x$ follow the Appell sequence property, such as $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}E^{(C)}_n(x, y) = nE^{(C)}_{n-1}(x, y)$ (p. 97).
- Shift Properties: For $n \geq 1$, it is shown that $B^{(C)}_n(x+1, y) - B^{(C)}_n(x, y) = nC_{n-1}(x, y)$, where $C_n$ represents the cosine expansion coefficients (p. 100).
- Explicit Representations: The authors express these polynomials in terms of Bernoulli numbers ($B_n$) and Euler numbers ($E_n$) using summation formulas, such as $E^{(C)}_n(y) = \sum_{m=0}^{[n/2]} \binom{n}{2m} (-1)^m y^{2m} E_{n-2m}$ (p. 101).

**Conclusion**
The study concludes by expressing the general polynomials $E^{(C)}_n(x, y)$ and $E^{(S)}_n(x, y)$ as linear combinations of their respective $y$-dependent base polynomials using the binomial theorem (p. 101). These results provide a structured framework for analyzing the behavior and zeros of these trigonometric-related polynomial sequences.

This paper explores generalizations of asymptotic equivalence for set sequences by incorporating the concepts of $W$-asymptotic $J_\lambda$-statistical equivalence of order $\alpha$, strongly statistical equivalence, and Cesàro summability.

**Key Concepts and Definitions**
The study operates within a metric space $(X, \rho)$ and utilizes an admissible ideal $J$ on the set of natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$. Key definitions introduced or extended include:

*   **W-asymptotically $J_\lambda$-statistically equivalent of order $\alpha$:** For $0 < \alpha \le 1$, two set sequences $\{A_k\}$ and $\{B_k\}$ are equivalent to $L$ if for every $\epsilon, \delta > 0$ and $x \in X$, the set $\{n \in \mathbb{N} : \frac{1}{\lambda_n^\alpha} |\{k \in I_n : |\frac{\rho(x, A_k)}{\rho(x, B_k)} - L| \ge \epsilon\}| \ge \delta\}$ belongs to $J$, where $I_n = [n - \lambda_n + 1, n]$ (p. 114).
*   **W-strongly asymptotically $J_\lambda$-statistically equivalent of order $\alpha$:** Two sequences defined via a positive real sequence $p = (p_k)$ are equivalent to $L$ if the set $\{n \in \mathbb{N} : \frac{1}{\lambda_n^\alpha} \sum_{k \in I_n} |\frac{\rho(x, A_k)}{\rho(x, B_k)} - L|^{p_k} \ge \delta\}$ belongs to $J$ (p. 115).
*   **W-strongly Cesáro asymptotically $J$-statistically equivalent of order $\alpha$:** This involves the condition $\{n \in \mathbb{N} : \frac{1}{n^\alpha} \sum_{k=1}^n |\frac{\rho(x, A_k)}{\rho(x, B_k)} - L|^{p_k} \ge \delta\} \in J$ (p. 114).

**Key Findings**
*   **Relationship between Equivalence Types:** The authors establish containment relations between the defined spaces based on the limit behavior of $\frac{\lambda_n^\alpha}{n^\alpha}$ (p. 115). 
*   **Role of the $p$ Sequence:** When $p = (p_k)$ is a constant sequence $(p)$, the authors prove that $W$-strongly asymptotically $J_\lambda$-statistical equivalence implies $W$-asymptotically $J_\lambda$-statistical equivalence, with the converse holding under the condition that the sequences are bounded (p. 116).
*   **Innovation:** The study extends previous work by Savas [21] by introducing the sequence $\lambda = (\lambda_n)$ and the power sequence $p = (p_k)$ into the definition of $W$-asymptotic equivalence, allowing for a more granular analysis of convergence properties (p. 114).

This paper investigates the foundations of the walk-on-spheres (WOS) algorithm for the Yukawa Partial Differential Equation (PDE), which is defined as:
$$\Delta u(x) = \mu^2 u(x), \quad \mu^2 \geq 0$$

The key points of the research are summarized below:

**1. Panharmonic Measure**
The authors define a $\mu$-panharmonic measure as a generalization of the classical harmonic measure. This measure is essential for solving the Dirichlet problem for the Yukawa equation in both small and regular domains (p. 170).

**2. Relationships with Brownian Motion**
The text establishes three key stochastic representations for the panharmonic measure:
- Hitting Measure: A variation of the Kakutani connection where the panharmonic measure relates to the expected hitting distribution of a Brownian motion (p. 172).
- Exponentially Killed Brownian Motion: The measure can be represented by a Brownian motion $W_{\mu}$ that is killed at a rate associated with the parameter $\mu^2$ (p. 174).
- Duffin Correspondence: A new interpretation involving an "escaping Brownian motion." By constructing an $(n+1)$-dimensional auxiliary domain $D \times I$, the authors demonstrate that the panharmonic solution can be mapped to a higher-dimensional harmonic problem (p. 174-175).

**3. Equivalence and Properties**
The study proves that $\mu$-panharmonic measures are mutually equivalent to classical harmonic measures. Furthermore, for $\mu^2 > 0$, the constraint that a domain must be "small" to guarantee a unique solution in the Dirichlet problem can be relaxed (p. 170, 175).

**4. Applications**
These theoretical advancements provide the mathematical justification for Monte Carlo simulations using the WOS algorithm. By establishing these stochastic connections, the authors facilitate better computational methods for approximating solutions to the Yukawa and related Helmholtz and Schrödinger equations (p. 171).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Based on the article 'New Bell–Sheffer Polynomial Sets', what is the fundamental purpose of the 'monomiality principle' described by the authors?
options: To replace linear algebra methods with purely visual geometric proofs.
To derive properties of Sheffer polynomial families directly from the elements of their generating functions.
To compute the numerical values of Bell numbers using only logarithmic iterations.
To identify the geographical distribution of researchers in the special issue.
questions.explanation: The text states that the method used traces back to results by Dattoli and Ben Cheikh on the monomiality principle, 'showing the possibility to derive explicitly the main properties of Sheffer polynomial families starting from the basic elements of their generating functions.'

questions.question: According to the editorial by H. M. Srivastava, what is the primary indicator of the 'status' of a special issue in the journal Axioms?
options: The total number of citations received by each individual article.
The specific country of origin for the majority of the Special Issue Editor's previous publications.
Quantitative metrics including publication count, rejection rates, and average processing time.
The number of honorary D.Sc. degrees held by the contributors.
questions.explanation: The Editorial section clearly lists the status of the special issue using metrics: 1. Publications, 2. Rejections, 3. Article Average Processing Time, and 4. Article Type.

questions.question: How are the higher order Bell numbers (b[r]_n) related to the functions E_r(t) introduced in the paper?
options: They are the coefficients of the Taylor expansion of E_r(t).
They are the compositional inverses of the E_r(t) functions.
They are equal to the derivative of the E_r(t) function with respect to r.
They represent the limits of the series E_r(t) as t approaches infinity.
questions.explanation: Remark 2 explicitly states: 'the coefficients of the Taylor expansion of Er (t) are given by the higher order Bell numbers b[r]_n' where E_r(t) = sum (b[r]_n * t^n / n!).

questions.question: In the context of the Sheffer polynomial definition provided, what condition must be met by the series g(t)?
options: g(t) must be a periodic function with a period of 2π.
g(t) must be an invertible series with g_0 not equal to zero.
g(t) must equal the identity function for all t.
g(t) must be a polynomial of a higher order than the Bell numbers.
questions.explanation: Section 2 defines the Sheffer polynomials using the pair (g(t), f(t)), where g(t) is defined as an invertible series with g_0 not equal to 0.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the functions E_r(t) and Λ_r(t) as established in Remark 1?
options: They are unrelated functions used for different types of series.
They are compositional inverses of each other (E_r(Λ_r(t)) = t and Λ_r(E_r(t)) = t).
E_r(t) is always greater than Λ_r(t) for all t > 0.
E_r(t) is the derivative of Λ_r(t) multiplied by a constant.
questions.explanation: Remark 1 lists several identities, the first of which is 'for all integers r, k, h, Er (Λ r (t)) = t, Λ r (Er (t)) = t'.

cards.front: Who is the Special Issue Editor for the 'Mathematical Analysis and Applications' volume of the journal Axioms?
What is the primary academic affiliation of the Special Issue Editor, Hari Mohan Srivastava?
cards.back: Professor Emeritus in the Department of Mathematics and Statistics at the University of Victoria, Canada

cards.front: How are Sheffer polynomials {sn(x)} defined via an exponential generating function?
cards.back: A(t) exp(xH(t)) = sum from n=0 to infinity [(sn(x) t^n) / n!], where A(t) = sum an(t^n/n!) and H(t) = sum hn(t^n/n!)

cards.front: What does the 'monomiality principle' allow researchers to do with Sheffer polynomial families?
cards.back: It allows for the explicit derivation of the main properties of Sheffer polynomial families starting from the basic elements of their generating functions.

cards.front: How are higher order Bell numbers b[r]n defined in relation to Er(t)?
cards.back: Er(t) = sum from n=1 to infinity [(b[r]n * t^n) / n!]

cards.front: What is the relationship between the functions Er(t) and Λr(t)?
cards.back: They are compositional inverses: Er(Λr(t)) = t and Λr(Er(t)) = t

cards.front: What are logarithmic numbers l[1]n representing symbolically?
cards.back: They are defined by the Taylor expansion coefficients of Λ0(t) = log(t+1), given by l[1]n = (-1)^(n-1) * (n-1)!

cards.front: What is the generating function G(t, x) for the Bell–Sheffer polynomials E^(1)_k(x)?
cards.back: G(t, x) = exp[(1 + x)E0(x)] = sum from k=0 to infinity [(E^(1)_k(x) * t^k) / k!]

cards.front: What were the keywords suggested for the call for papers in this Special Issue?
cards.back: Mathematical (or Higher Transcendental) Functions, Fractional Calculus, q-Series and q-Polynomials, Analytic Number Theory, Special Functions of Mathematical Physics, and Geometric Function Theory of Complex Analysis.

cards.front: What is the primary license under which articles in the Axioms Special Issue are distributed?

slides.title: Mathematical Analysis and Applications
bullets: Overview of diverse research in mathematical analysis.
Special issue published in Axioms (2018).
Features applications from oscillation theory to fractional calculus.
Covers theoretical advancements and practical modeling.
slides.notes: This special issue gathers recent advancements across various fields within mathematical analysis. It highlights the interdisciplinary nature of modern mathematical research.

slides.title: New Bell–Sheffer Polynomial Sets
bullets: Explores Sheffer and Brenke polynomial families.
Introduces Bell–Sheffer polynomials using iteration.
Applies the monomiality principle and shift operators.
Connects to higher-order Bell and logarithmic numbers.
slides.notes: The integration of Bell numbers into Sheffer polynomial theory showcases the power of the monomiality principle in deriving polynomial properties.

slides.title: Oscillatory Acoustic Vacuum Models
bullets: Studies in-plane oscillations in particle lattices.
Uses Melnikov-type analysis for periodic persistence.
Analyzes non-linear low-energy acoustic vacuum systems.
Investigates harmonic excitation and symmetry.
slides.notes: These researchers demonstrate how Melnikov analysis effectively predicts periodic solutions in complex nonlinear mechanical systems.

slides.title: Abel’s Integral Equations in Distributions
bullets: Studies fractional calculus within distributional spaces.
Solves generalized Abel's integral equations.
Utilizes inverse convolutional operators and Babenko's approach.
Applied to viscoelastic systems and non-local dynamics.
slides.notes: Moving beyond classical calculus, this approach solves integral equations in a broader mathematical space, aiding problems in physics and engineering.

slides.title: Interior Regularity for Degenerate Elliptic Equations
bullets: Proves Schauder estimates for degenerate equations.
Links Marchaud fractional derivatives to extension problems.
Addresses mixed Dirichlet–Neumann boundary conditions.
Enables classical analysis on non-local operators.
slides.notes: By transforming the non-local derivative into a local degenerate extension problem, researchers can apply powerful interior regularity techniques.

slides.title: Umbral Methods and Harmonic Numbers
bullets: Examines harmonic numbers via umbral operational methods.
Reduces complex calculations to algebraic manipulations.
Introduces the Harmonic-Based Exponential Function (HBEF).
Simplifies series involving harmonic number indices.
slides.notes: The umbral approach transforms the harmonic-number index into a power, streamlining otherwise tedious analytical computations.

slides.title: Control in Zika Virus Epidemic Models
bullets: Models Zika virus with various transmission modes.
Proposes sub-optimal controls for disease reduction.
Uses Differential Evolution (DE) for optimization.
Prioritizes vector elimination as a key strategy.
slides.notes: Mathematical modeling provides actionable insights for public health, helping design effective intervention strategies against epidemics.

slides.title: Identities for Euler and Bernoulli Polynomials
bullets: Investigates Appell sequences and special polynomials.
Defines new cosine/sine-Euler and Bernoulli polynomials.
Provides explicit identities and generating functions.
Analyzes polynomial zeros via computational methods.
slides.notes: These identities extend classical polynomial theory, providing new tools for studying special functions through a unified Appell sequence framework.

slides.title: Statistical Equivalence and Pre-Metric Spaces
bullets: Extends statistical equivalence to J-statistical forms.
Introduces W-asymptotically Jλ-statistical concepts.
Incorporates sequence of positive numbers for generality.
Investigates convergence in ideal-based settings.
slides.notes: This work generalizes convergence theory, creating robust definitions adaptable to various metric and ideal-based mathematical structures.

slides.title: Yukawa Potential and Panharmonic Measure
bullets: Analyzes Yukawa equation via panharmonic measure.
Utilizes Brownian motion and stochastic representations.
Establishes equivalence between harmonic/pan-harmonic measures.
Optimizes Walk-on-Spheres (WOS) simulation algorithms.
slides.notes: By linking the panharmonic measure to stochastic processes, mathematicians improve the efficiency of Monte Carlo simulations for physical PDEs.

config.title: Lesson 5: Mathematical Analysis and Applications - Presentation
